brief_description: Calendar-time/region–adjusted pooled logistic regression comparing ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 for risk of a positive SARS-CoV-2 test with a vaccine-specific time-varying effect.
paper_text: “We compared the effectiveness of a first dose of ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 using pooled logistic regression (PLR) … with time since vaccination as the timescale and with the outcome risk estimated each day. The effect is permitted to vary over the timescale to account for the potential time-varying differences in vaccine protection between the two brands.” [...] “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination. Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.” [...] “Standard errors for the PLR model were obtained using the clustered sandwich estimator to account for within-participant clustering. Confidence intervals for the vaccinespecific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method (that is, a first-order Taylor series approximation of the variance).” [...] “Three models were fit for each outcome, with progressive adjustment for confounders 1 : adjusting for region-specific calendartime effects, by including a two-knot restricted cubic spline for the date of vaccination and its interaction with region …” [...] “PLR models are computationally expensive to fit … a sampling strategy was used such that all those who experienced the event of interest are selected (to retain statistical power), and a random sample of 50 000 event-free participants are selected. Persontime is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability to recover the characteristics of the complete cohort.” [...] “Using an intention-to-treat approach, comparative effectiveness estimates beyond 14 weeks were considered to be second-dose effects.”
explanation: The code implements daily-risk PLR with a vaccine-specific spline whose knots are based on event times, uses clustered sandwich SEs, delta-method CIs for marginal curves, and samples 50,000 event-free with inverse-probability weights, matching the paper’s parameter specifications. Region-specific calendar-time adjustment is included via a spline-by-region interaction as described.

brief_description: Calendar-time/region–adjusted pooled logistic regression comparing ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 for risk of a positive SARS-CoV-2 test with a vaccine-specific time-varying effect.
paper_text: Confidence intervals for the vaccinespecific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method (that is, a first-order Taylor series approximation of the variance). [...] By six weeks (42 days) post-vaccination, before receipt of the second dose for the vast majority of participants, the ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 absolute risk difference per 1000 people (fig 2) for a positive SARS-CoV-2 test was -0.24 (95% confidence interval -1.71 to 1.22)

explanation: The paper specifies delta-method confidence intervals and reports 95% CIs. The code computes SEs for cumulative measures and constructs 95% CIs using qnorm(0.025/0.975), matching the paper’s inference approach.

brief_description: Calendar-time/region–adjusted pooled logistic regression comparing ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 for risk of a positive SARS-CoV-2 test with a vaccine-specific time-varying effect.
paper_text: The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination. Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.[...]adjusting for region-specific calendartime effects, by including a two-knot restricted cubic spline for the date of vaccination and its interaction with region
code_text:
vax1_az = (vax1_type=="az")*1[...]formula_timesincevax_ns <- . ~ . + vax1_az + vax1_az:nsevents(tstop, outcome_event, 4)[...]nsevents <- function(x, events, df){
  # this is the same as the `ns` function,
  # except the knot locations are chosen
  # based on the event times only, not on all person-time
  probs <- seq(0,df)/df
  q <- quantile(x[events==1], probs=probs)
  ns(x, knots=q[-c(1, df+1)], Boundary.knots = q[c(1, df+1)])
}[...]formula_spacetime <- . ~ . + ns(vax1_day, 3)*region
explanation: The paper specifies a vaccine-specific restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination with knots based on event-time quartiles and a spline-by-region interaction. The code codes vaccine as 0/1 (vax1_az), interacts it with a spline built from event-time quantiles via nsevents(), and includes ns(vax1_day, 3)*region.

paper_text: “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination. Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.” [...] “Three models were fit for each outcome, with progressive adjustment for confounders 1 : adjusting for region-specific calendar-time effects, by including a two-knot restricted cubic spline for the date of vaccination and its interaction with region 2 ; additionally adjusting for demographic characteristics 3 ; additionally adjusting for clinical characteristics.” [...] “Standard errors for the PLR model were obtained using the clustered sandwich estimator to account for within-participant clustering.” [...] “Confidence intervals for the vaccine-specific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method (that is, a first-order Taylor series approximation of the variance).” [...] “a sampling strategy was used such that all those who experienced the event of interest are selected (to retain statistical power), and a random sample of 50 000 event-free participants are selected. explanation: The code implements event-time-based spline knots (df=4 giving three internal knots), region-by-calendar-time adjustment, clustered sandwich standard errors, delta-method variances for marginal curves, and sampling of 50,000 non-events with inverse-probability weights, matching the paper’s parameter settings. paper_text: “Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.” [...] “Standard errors for the PLR model were obtained using the clustered sandwich estimator to account for within-participant clustering.” [...] “Confidence intervals for the vaccinespecific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method (that is, a first-order Taylor series approximation of the variance).” [...] “PLR models are computationally expensive to fit, as the input dataset must be arranged as one row per person per day of follow-up. To manage this, a sampling strategy was used such that all those who experienced the event of interest are selected (to retain statistical power), and a random sample of 50 000 event-free participants are selected. Persontime is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability to recover the characteristics of the complete cohort.”
code_text:
“nsevents <- function(x, events, df){
  # this is the same as the `ns` function,
  # except the knot locations are chosen
  # based on the event times only, not on all person-time
  probs <- seq(0,df)/df
  q <- quantile(x[events==1], probs=probs)
  ns(x, knots=q[-c(1, df+1)], Boundary.knots = q[c(1, df+1)])
}” [...] “if(timescale=="timesincevax"){ [...] formula_timescale_ns <- . ~ . + nsevents(tstop, outcome_event, 4) # spline for timescale only [...] formula_spacetime <- . ~ . + ns(vax1_day, 3)*region # spline for space-time adjustments [...] formula_timesincevax_ns <- . ~ . + vax1_az + vax1_az:nsevents(tstop, outcome_event, 4) }” [...] “robustSEs <- lmtest::coeftest(model, vcov. = sandwich::vcovCL(model, cluster = cluster, type = "HC0"))” [...] “vcov <- vcovCL(plrmod, cluster = cluster, type = "HC0")” [...] “cmlinc_variance <- function(model, vcov, newdata, id, time, weights){

  # calculate variance of adjusted survival / adjusted cumulative incidence” [...] “# partial derivative of cumulative incidence at t” [...] “variance <- rowSums(crossprod(t(partial_derivative), vcov) * partial_derivative) # t x 1” [...] “sample_outcome = sample_nonoutcomes_n(!is.na(tte_outcome), patient_id, samplesize_nonoutcomes_n),

    sample_weights = sample_weights(!is.na(tte_outcome), sample_outcome),” [...] “plrmod3 <- parglm(
  formula = formula3_pw,
  data = data_plr,
  weights = sample_weights,
  family = binomial,
  control = parglmparams,
  na.action = "na.fail",
  model = FALSE
)” [...] “parglmparams <- parglm.control(
  method = "LINPACK",
  nthreads = 8,
  maxit = 40 # default = 25
)” [...] “action_model("covidadmitted", "timesincevax", "1", "plr", 50000),”
explanation: The code implements event-time-based natural spline knots with df=4 (yielding quartiles), clustered sandwich standard errors, delta-method style variance for marginal cumulative incidence, and sampling 50,000 non-events with inverse-probability weights, matching the paper’s parameter specifications. It also adjusts calendar time by region via a spline interaction as described.

brief_description: Sensitivity analysis: fully adjusted pooled logistic regression restricted to participants with no prior SARS-CoV-2 infection for outcome of unplanned covid-19–related hospital admission.
paper_text: “We conducted a post-hoc sensitivity analysis that repeated the main analysis in the subgroup of participants who had no evidence of a prior SARS-CoV-2 infection.” [...] “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination. Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.” [...] “Standard errors for the PLR model were obtained using the clustered sandwich estimator to account for within-participant clustering.” [...] “Confidence intervals for the vaccinespecific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method (that is, a first-order Taylor series approximation of the variance).” [...] “To manage this, a sampling strategy was used such that all those who experienced the event of interest are selected (to retain statistical power), and a random sample of 50 000 event-free participants are selected. Persontime is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability to recover the characteristics of the complete cohort.”
code_text:
data_cohort <- read_rds(here("output", "data", "data_cohort.rds")) %>%
  filter(
    prior_covid_infection==0 | (exclude_prior_infection!=1L)
  ) [...] nsevents <- function(x, events, df){
  # this is the same as the `ns` function,
  # except the knot locations are chosen
  # based on the event times only, not on all person-time
  probs <- seq(0,df)/df
  q <- quantile(x[events==1], probs=probs)
  ns(x, knots=q[-c(1, df+1)], Boundary.knots = q[c(1, df+1)])
} [...] formula_timesincevax_ns <- . ~ . + vax1_az + vax1_az:nsevents(tstop, outcome_event, 4) [...] plrmod3 <- parglm(
  formula = formula3_ns,
  data = data_plr,
  weights = sample_weights,
  family = binomial,
  control = parglmparams,
  na.action = "na.fail",
  model = FALSE
) [...] vcov <- vcovCL(plrmod, cluster = cluster, type = "HC0") [...] sample_weights <- function(had_outcome, sampled){
  # `had_outcome` is a boolean indicating if the subject has experienced the outcome or not
  # `sampled` is a boolean indicating if the patient is to be sampled or not
  case_when(
    had_outcome ~ 1,
    !had_outcome & !sampled ~ 0,
    !had_outcome & sampled ~ sum(!had_outcome)/sum((sampled) & !had_outcome),
    TRUE ~ NA_real_
  )
} [...] "Confidence intervals for the risk-adjusted survival rates are calculated using the delta method (i.e, a first-order Taylor series approximation of the variance of the cumulative incidence)." [...] action_model("covidadmitted", "timesincevax", "1", "plr", 50000, "1"),
explanation: The code implements the sensitivity restriction and uses event-time-based spline knots, clustered sandwich standard errors, delta-method CIs, and sampling 50,000 non-events with inverse-probability weights, matching the paper’s parameter settings. These parameters apply to the fully adjusted PLR used for the covidadmitted outcome.
